data.title: Capital One falls 6% after missing on both top- and bottom-line in Q1
data.description: MCLEAN, Va. - Capital One Financial Corporation (NYSE:COF) reported first quarter 2026 results that fell short of analyst expectations, with adjusted earnings per share of $4.42 missing the consensus estimate of $4.51 by $0.09. Revenue of $15.2 billion also came in below the $15.4 billion analyst estimate.Shares fell 6.7% in after hours trading following the results.The company posted net income of $2.2 billion, or $3.34 per diluted share, compared to $1.4 billion, or $3.45 per share, in the first quarter of 2025. Total net revenue decreased 2% from the prior quarter to $15.2 billion but rose 52% YoY. The quarter included $477 million in Discover amortization expenses and $415 million in integration expenses."Our results in the first quarter reflect solid top line growth and strong credit performance," said Richard D. Fairbank, Founder, Chairman, and Chief Executive Officer. "The Discover integration continues to go well and we continue to build momentum from this game-changing acquisition."Pre-provision earnings increased 8% to $6.8 billion, while the provision for credit losses decreased $74 million to $4.1 billion. Net charge-offs totaled $3.8 billion with a $230 million loan reserve build. The net interest margin declined 39 basis points to 7.87%.Period-end loans held for investment decreased 1% to $447.8 billion, with credit card loans down 3% to $270.6 billion. Total deposits increased 3% to $489.1 billion. The company’s common equity Tier 1 capital ratio stood at 14.4% under the Basel III Standardized Approach at quarter end.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Adobe announces $25 billion stock buyback
data.description: April 21 (Reuters) - Adobe on Tuesday said its board of directors has approved a new $25 billion stock repurchase program through April 30, 2030, sending its shares up around 2% in extended trading. The company’s shares have fallen around 30% so far this year as investors weigh the outcome of new agentic tools that many fear could hamper demand for traditional software and design products such as those provided by Adobe. "Our new $25 billion share repurchase authorization is a direct expression of confidence in our robust cash flow and the long-term value we are delivering to investors," said Adobe CFO Dan Durn.

data.title: Fed chair pick Warsh makes case for smaller Fed holdings in hearing
data.description: NEW YORK, April 21 (Reuters) - Kevin Warsh, tapped by President Donald Trump to lead the Federal Reserve, told a Senate panel on Tuesday he would work with the Treasury Department to help achieve his goal of a smaller Fed balance sheet, in an effort observers say would represent a long-term project for the central bank. “Working with the Treasury Secretary, we’re going to have to find a way in which we can take the balance sheet and make it smaller,” he said as part of his confirmation hearing to succeed current Fed Chair Jerome Powell. Warsh’s opposition to large-sized Fed holdings rests on a few fronts: He thinks it benefits Wall Street over Main Street, while forcing the Fed to keep higher short-term rates than would otherwise be the case. He also thinks that while using bond buying during the financial crisis nearly two decades ago was defensible, recent use cases aren’t. “The big balance sheet has become an ordinary, recurring force" and "has been quite unhelpful, and is part of the reason why the Fed is in the business of politics,” Warsh said. If Fed holdings were smaller, “I think interest rates could be lower, inflation could be better, and the economy could be stronger,” he said. Warsh remained largely aspirational on how he’d get Fed holdings down, but he said whatever the Fed would do under his command would be well communicated and done “slowly and deliberatively.” Warsh’s comments on the central bank’s extensive holdings of cash, bonds and other assets are among his first of real substance on an issue that’s become core to central bank monetary policy making. BIG BALANCE SHEET Since the financial crisis, the Fed has used purchases of Treasury and mortgage bonds to help calm financial markets in times of high stress and to provide stimulus to the economy when the interest rate target is near zero and can be cut no further. This system and the tools to achieve it have seen Fed holdings go from under a trillion dollars in the run-up to the financial crisis starting in 2007 to a peak of $9 trillion in 2022, as the Fed navigated the COVID-19 pandemic. Fed holdings now stand at $6.7 trillion and a recent New York Fed report projected that based on technical factors Fed holdings could hit $10 trillion by the end of 2035. Most Fed officials are unconcerned by the size of Fed holdings. For them, the most critical issue is that the rate control system operated by the central bank works very well, while robust levels of liquidity in the financial system help protect against shocks. The case against a large Fed balance sheet has centered on a few concerns. For one, large holdings have driven the Fed into a loss-making position it is still trying to dig out of, and while that does not affect Fed operations, there are concerns it could become a political problem for the central bank at some point. Others worry large Fed holdings of bonds distort markets and make the central bank too big a player in what would normally be private markets. If Warsh is confirmed as Fed chair, his efforts to cut Fed holdings would challenge an interest rate management toolkit that effectively limits how far the central bank can reduce its holdings and maintain strong control over its interest rate target. COORDINATION ASPECT NOT YET CLEAR Market participants said Warsh hasn’t yet made clear how the Fed and Treasury would work together on the balance sheet. Derek Tang, an analyst at research firm LH Meyer, said he believes “Warsh would like Treasury and the Fed to communicate more clearly to each other what their respective plans” over Treasury debt issuance, and to better coordinate their respective positions. Observers doubt however that Warsh would push the Fed to sell Treasury debt. “Warsh certainly seemed to suggest a gradual approach to decreasing the balance sheet, which to me suggests that outright asset sales are unlikely,” said Gennadiy Goldberg, head of U.S. rates strategy  at TD Securities. FRAMEWORK FORMING Over recent weeks an effort inside and outside the Fed has laid out a potential pathway to reduce the Fed’s balance sheet, and that emerging framework argues easing liquidity regulations and doing more to induce usage of central bank liquidity facilities would reduce demand for bank reserves, in turn allowing the Fed to hold fewer assets. Some have also argued that a smaller Fed balance sheet would lead to higher long-term rates and that this increase in restraint would allow policymakers to cut their interest rate target to balance that out. New York Fed President John Williams told reporters at the end of March that higher long-term rates resulting from a smaller Fed balance sheet theoretically opens the door to lower short-term rates to offset those headwinds, but he cautioned it is hard to measure in advance how much lower the central bank rate target could go.

data.title: Chubb beats Q1 profit estimates on strong underwriting performance
data.description: ZURICH - Chubb Limited (NYSE:CB) reported first-quarter adjusted earnings per share of $6.82, surpassing the analyst estimate of $6.60, as the insurer posted strong underwriting results and record investment income. Shares rose 0.2% after hours following the results.Net premiums written rose 10.7% to $14.01 billion, exceeding the consensus estimate of $13.51 billion. Net income reached $2.32 billion, or $5.88 per share, up 74.3% from the prior year, which was impacted by $1.47 billion in losses from California wildfires. Core operating income climbed 80.6% to $2.69 billion. Revenue increased 12.1% YoY from $12.00 billion in the first quarter of 2025. P&C underwriting income surged 306.3% to $1.79 billion, with a combined ratio of 84.0%, compared to 95.7% in the prior year. Current accident year underwriting income excluding catastrophe losses increased 9.8% to $2.01 billion, with a combined ratio of 82.1%. Total pre-tax net catastrophe losses were $500 million, down from $1.64 billion last year."We had an excellent quarter and start to the year, which speaks to the strength and resilience of our company in a period of elevated uncertainty," said Evan G. Greenberg, Chairman and CEO.P&C net premiums written grew 7.2% to $11.72 billion, with consumer insurance up 14.2% and commercial insurance up 4.6%. North America increased 4.1%, while Overseas General rose 14.4%, or 6.1% in constant dollars. Life Insurance net premiums written jumped 33.1% to $2.29 billion, and segment income increased 8.5% to $316 million.Pre-tax net investment income rose 9.5% to $1.71 billion, while adjusted net investment income reached a record $1.84 billion, up 10.1%. Annualized core operating return on tangible equity was 20.6%, compared to 13.0% in the prior year.This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

data.title: Purdue Pharma’s $5.5 billion sentencing for opioid charges delayed after victims show up to court
data.description: By Dietrich Knauth NEW YORK, April 21 (Reuters) - A federal judge on Tuesday delayed the planned criminal sentencing of Purdue Pharma by one week, saying she wanted to allow more public participation before concluding a criminal case over the company’s marketing of addictive opioid drugs. U.S. District Judge Madeline Cox Arleo was expected to accept Purdue’s 2020 guilty plea at a court hearing in Newark, New Jersey, which would have imposed a $3.5 billion criminal fine and $2 billion in criminal forfeiture against the company for its role in fueling an opioid epidemic that has claimed more than 1 million lives in the U.S. since 2000.     Arleo initially scheduled the hearing to occur on Zoom, but changed course during the proceedings and delayed it until April 28 after protesters and members of the public showed up at court to participate in the sentencing hearing. One woman who joined the hearing remotely shouted, "This is not justice!" while the judge was speaking.  "In this plea, the single most important people that should be heard are the victims," Arleo said. "If the public and victims wish to be heard in person, I’m going to accommodate that." Purdue did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The U.S. Department of Justice declined to comment on the delay. The sentencing is one of the last steps before Purdue can complete a bankruptcy settlement that would dissolve the company and use its assets to pay $7.4 billion to those harmed by the opioid crisis.  Eight victims of the opioid crisis were scheduled to speak at the hearing, sharing their personal stories of suffering, loss and addiction. Some of the statements that were filed in court urged the judge to reject a plea deal that let company owners and executives escape prosecution.  Purdue reached the plea deal in 2020. Purdue admitted to charges that it aggressively marketed its products to doctors who were diverting the drugs for illegal use, defrauding the government by avoiding controls meant to reduce illegal opioid use, and paying kickbacks to doctors to boost its opioid sales. The company previously pleaded guilty to criminal charges in 2007, admitting that it misled doctors and federal regulators about OxyContin’s risk of addiction and misuse. Most of the criminal fines will go unpaid under an agreement with the Justice Department that allows Purdue to direct its remaining assets to repaying creditors, mostly state and local governments, which were left to deal with the cost and consequences of the opioid epidemic. The federal government waived its right to repayment for all but $225 million of the fines and penalties to allow Purdue to devote its assets to paying other opioid creditors, in the deal the Justice Department reached with Purdue after the company filed for bankruptcy in 2019.  Most of the Purdue settlement money will go to states and local governments, which have agreed to use the money for opioid abatement efforts such as addiction treatment. Purdue’s owners, members of the Sackler family, are contributing at least $6.5 ​billion to fund the bankruptcy ​settlement. Purdue is one of many drug manufacturers, distributors, pharmacy operators and others who have collectively in recent years agreed to pay about $57 billion to resolve lawsuits and investigations by states and local governments accusing them of helping fuel a deadly opioid addiction epidemic in the U.S.

data.title: Google Cloud Next: BofA expects ‘confident tone’
data.description: Bank of America expects Google/Alphabet (GOOGL) to strike an upbeat tone at its annual Cloud Next conference this week, with analysts flagging agent-based AI, custom silicon progress and enterprise client wins as the key themes for investors to watch.The three-day event runs from April 22 to 24 in Las Vegas, with Google Cloud CEO keynote remarks on day one expected to cover infrastructure advancements and agentic capabilities. BofA stated that Google has signaled the conference will focus on "building blocks for the future: Agentic AI, modern infrastructure and hands on problem solving."On infrastructure, BofA highlighted Google’s custom tensor processing units as a key differentiator, with potential announcements around next-generation TPU capabilities, the timing of Rubin GPU availability on Google Cloud and improvements to AI networking. The bank expects Google to showcase a broad list of enterprise TPU adopters, including leading AI developers such as OpenAI and Anthropic.At the platform level, BofA said expanded Gemini capabilities, potentially including a Gemini 3.5 release, enhancements to Vertex AI and improved multi-agent deployment frameworks could all feature. On applications, the bank believes this year’s event is centered on "production-ready AI systems," with domain-specific and enterprise agents among likely announcements.BofA estimates Google Cloud revenues will grow from $59 billion in 2025 to $116 billion by 2027, with profits expanding by $21 billion over the two-year period and contributing 38% of Alphabet’s total profit growth. Applying a 10 times revenue multiple on 2027 cloud revenue "yields a potential $1.2tr in value contribution," analysts wrote.BofA maintains a buy rating on Alphabet with a $370 price target.

data.title: Amazon to invest up to $25 bn in Anthropic to expand partnership; shares rise
data.description:
Investing.com - Shares of Amazon rose by more than 2% in premarket trading on Tuesday, after the e-commerce giant said it will invest up to $25 billion in AI startup Anthropic.
The move expands an existing relationship between the companies, with Amazon having already invested $8 billion in the Anthropic, the creator of the popular Claude AI model.
Anthropic has also promised to spend over $100 billion over the next decade on Amazon’s cloud infrastructure, securing up to 5 gigawatts of capacity using Amazon’s Trainium chips to power Claude.
Under the terms of the investment, Amazon will plug $5 billion into Anthropic immediately, and an additional $20 billion in the future, tied to commercial milestones.  
[[pro_promotion |
Get more market insights with InvestingPro - now 55% off ]]
The partnership includes access to Trainium2, Trainium3, Trainium4, and future chip generations. Anthropic will also utilize tens of millions of Amazon’s Graviton CPU cores. The collaboration encompasses Trainium3 capacity expected to come online this year and expanded international inference capabilities in Asia and Europe, the company’s statement said. 
"We view the expanded partnership between [Amazon Web Services] and Anthropic announced this afternoon positively as it deepens Amazon’s relationship with Anthropic and shows that Trainium is gaining momentum and share of AI training and inference," Truist Securities analyst Youssef Squali said in a note. 
Earlier this month, Amazon CEO Andy Jassy said in a company shareholder letter that Amazon’s custom chip business is growing rapidly, as large tech firms are starting to make their own processors to reduce dependence on semiconductor giants like Nvidia. The letter said that the business has doubled its annualized revenue run rate to over $20 billion, up ​from the $10 billion the company disclosed alongside its fourth-quarter results.
Jassy also hinted towards future chip sales to other firms by saying that “[t]here’s so much demand for our chips that it’s quite possible we’ll sell racks of them to third parties in the future.”
AWS customers will gain access to Claude directly through their existing AWS accounts, eliminating the need for additional credentials or billing relationships. The platform will integrate with existing AWS access controls and monitoring systems.
The companies have previously collaborated on Project Rainier, one of the world’s largest AI compute clusters with nearly half a million Trainium2 chips. More than 100,000 customers currently run Claude models on AWS through Amazon Bedrock.
"Anthropic’s commitment to run its large language models on AWS Trainium for the next decade reflects the progress we’ve made together on custom silicon," Jassy said.
Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei said the collaboration will allow the company "to continue advancing AI research while delivering Claude to our customers, including the more than 100,000 building on AWS."
Amazon is set to report its first-quarter results on April 29. Stifel analysts said the tech giant "remains one of our top picks and sentiment is elevated as we head into the print, which we expect to be solid across core ecomm, AWS, and advertising."
(Jaiveer Shekhawat and Scott Kanowsky contributed to this article)

data.title: Warsh at Senate hearing calls for Fed regime change, gets grilled about assets
data.description:
Investing.com - Kevin Warsh, President Donald Trump’s nominee for the next Federal Reserve Chair, on Tuesday at a Congressional confirmation hearing said "fundamental policy reforms" and "regime change" were needed at the central bank. Warsh was also grilled on his financial assets which he said he would sell before taking office.  
The hearing was closely watched for cues on major issues such as the Fed’s outlook on interest rates and the central bank’s independence. The proceedings were conducted by the U.S. Senate’s Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs. Watch here. 
[[pro_promotion |
Get more insights on monetary policy on InvestingPro
]]
Warsh notes ’legacy’ of policy errors
Warsh was nominated as the next chair at the end of January by Trump. At the time, market participants saw the choice as a safe one, based on Warsh’s hawkish tilt as a Fed Governor in the past. The U.S. dollar in particular had seen a noticeable move on odds that Warsh would be more likely to reduce the Fed’s balance sheet. However, Warsh’s recent comments have tilted towards advocating for cutting interest rates.
The former central bank governor said "fundamental policy reforms" and "regime change in the conduct of policy" were needed as the Fed was still dealing with a "legacy" of policy errors from 2021 and 2022. The Fed famously in 2021, under the leadership of current chief Jerome Powell, described rising inflation as "transitory" only to later abandon the term and initiate a series of rate hikes to combat soaring consumer prices.
Warsh said a different and new inflation framework was needed, adding that of the Fed’s two tools involving interest rates and balance sheet, the former was "fairer." Warsh also called for "new tools" and "new communications."
Warsh says Fed will be independent
The Senate hearing comes amid concerns about the Fed’s independence. Trump on Tuesday told CNBC that he would be "disappointed” if the new Fed chair does not cut rates. The president has long advocated for lower interest rates and has been involved in several high-profile spats with Powell over the issue.
Trump has called Powell names and mused about firing him. Meanwhile, the Department of Justice (DoJ) has opened an investigation into a Fed project involving the renovation of office buildings.
Powell in January, in an extraordinary move, said in a public statement that the DoJ probe and the “threat of criminal charges” was a “consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President.”
Warsh in his opening remarks at the Senate hearing said, if confirmed as chair, he would ensure that the Fed’s conduct remained "strictly independent."   
Later, when asked whether Trump had told him he would get the Fed job only if he committed to cut interest rates, Warsh said the president "never asks" him to "predetermine" or "fix" any interest rate decision.  
Grilled about financial assets
Warsh was also subjected to grilling about his financial assets at the hearing, in particular from U.S. Senator Elizabeth Warren. Last Tuesday, public financial disclosures showed that Warsh alone held assets worth about $135 million to $225 million, including holdings listed in two funds valued at over $50 million.
Warren noted the "more than $100 million" in investments Warsh had refused to disclose, and repeatedly asked him whether any of them were related to companies affiliated with Trump and his family, vehicles affiliated with money laundering, or with disgraced financier Jeffrey Epstein.
Warsh said he had agreed to sell all of his financial assets, "especially" those referenced by Warren. The former central bank governor said he had entered an agreement with the U.S. Office of Government Ethics to divest his assets within 90 days after being confirmed as Fed chair.       
Warsh’s confirmation has faced opposition from U.S. Senator Thom Tillis, a key Republican who has vowed to block his appointment pending the DoJ probe into Powell. Tillis at the hearing asked for clarity on Warsh’s agreement and whether or not he was out of compliance.   
Wall Street did not show any direct reaction to Warsh’s hearing after it got underway, as investors remain more focused on developments in the Middle East. Here are some popular exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index: SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trust (NYSE:SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSE:VOO), and iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (NYSE:IVV).

data.title: Oil rises, Brent gains about 3% amid increasing uncertainty over U.S.-Iran talks
data.description:
Investing.com - Oil prices ticked up on Tuesday, amid increasing uncertainty over the state of fresh U.S.-Iran talks before the expiry of a temporary ceasefire between the two countries later this week. 
At 14:33 ET (18:33 GMT), Brent oil futures, the global benchmark, were up 3% to $98.36 a barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures climbed 2.3% to $89.44 a barrel. 
[[pro_promotion |Get more oil market insights with InvestingPro - now 55% off]]
Iran calls U.S. blockade ’act of war’
Crude prices had risen sharply in the prior session, reversing steep losses notched last week, after tensions between the U.S. and Iran escalated over the weekend.
The U.S. seized an Iranian-flagged cargo ship, leading to a threat of retaliation from Tehran. Iran also once again shuttered the Strait of Hormuz, despite having reopened the vital waterway to commercial shipping traffic on Friday, citing an ongoing U.S. blockade of Iranian ports and coastline.
"Blockading Iranian ports is an act of war and thus a violation of the ceasefire. Striking a commercial vessel and taking its crew hostage is an even greater violation," Iran’s foreign minister Abbas Araghchi said on X. 
President Donald Trump on Monday had said the naval blockade would remain in place until a peace deal was reached, although the prospect of new discussions between the U.S. and Iran were mired in growing uncertainty.
VP Vance’s trip to Pakistan on hold
The New York Times reported that U.S. Vice President JD Vance was supposed to fly to Islamabad in Pakistan for more talks, but the trip was now on hold after Tehran failed to respond to U.S. negotiating positions, citing a U.S. official with direct knowledge of the matter.
Meanwhile, Iran’s Fars News Agency said the country was yet to decide on attending the talks, citing the foreign ministry’s spokesperson. State media also said that Tehran wanted the U.S. to lift its blockade before peace talks could restart.
Trump had earlier said that more negotiations with Iran were set to happen this week, with a delegation expected to reach Pakistan either on Tuesday or Wednesday. 
The U.S. president on Tuesday told CNBC said Iran had "no choice but to send people to talks" and that he thinks there will be a "great deal." 
"Ongoing uncertainty continues to overshadow any peace agreement, as Iran remains reluctant to attend a second round of talks in Pakistan," analysts at ANZ said in a note. 
Meanwhile, the clock is counting down to the end of a pause to the fighting later this week, although the exact timing of the deadline remained unspecified. Trump announced the two-week ceasefire on April 7 at 18:32 ET (22:32 GMT).
Trump told CNBC he did not want to extend the two-week ceasefire once it expired, and that the U.S. was "ready to go" to "be bombing Iran" if no deal was reached. On Monday, the president had told Bloomberg News that the expiration would be on Wednesday evening Washington time.
Hormuz shipping disruptions in focus
Crucially, shipping activity through the Strait of Hormuz, a conduit used by roughly a fifth of the world’s oil, has stayed all but closed.
Iran has effectively blocked the passage since the onset of the war in late February. While the initial oil price spike sparked by this disruption has tempered, crude is still well above pre-war levels. 
However, analysts at ANZ noted efforts by Saudi Arabia and UAE to bypass shipments through Hormuz. The countries were using the Yanbu and Fujairah terminals, located in the Red Sea and the Gulf of Oman, respectively, to load oil and avoid Hormuz.
ANZ said combined loading at the facilities had risen to 6.5 million barrels per day, up from 5.0 million bpd seen before the war. 
Ambar Warrick and Scott Kanowsky contributed to this article

data.title: Trump extends U.S.-Iran ceasefire even as further peace talks come to a standstill
data.description:
Investing.com - President Donald Trump on Tuesday said he would extend the ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran until such a time that Tehran would be able to present a unified negotiating proposal. The move came after media reports hinted at a breakdown in more planned peace talks between the two countries.
"Based on the fact that the Government of Iran is seriously fractured, not unexpectedly so and, upon the request of Field Marshal Asim Munir, and Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, of Pakistan, we have been asked to hold our Attack on the Country of Iran until such time as their leaders and representatives can come up with a unified proposal," the president said on his Truth Social service.
"I have therefore directed our Military to continue the Blockade and, in all other respects, remain ready and able, and will therefore extend the Ceasefire until such time as their proposal is submitted, and discussions are concluded, one way or the other," Trump added.
[[pro_promotion |
Keep tabs on developments in the Middle East with InvestingPro - now 55% off
]]
Earlier, market participants had been on edge amid growing uncertainty over the state of peace talks, ahead of a looming expiration of a two-week ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran on Wednesday.  
The New York Times reported in the afternoon that U.S. Vice President JD Vance’s trip to Islamabad in Pakistan for more talks was on hold after Tehran failed to respond to U.S. negotiating positions, citing a U.S. official with direct knowledge of the matter.
Iran’s Fars News Agency said the country was yet to decide on attending the talks, citing the foreign ministry’s spokesperson. State media also said that Tehran wanted the U.S. to lift its blockade on vessels entering and exiting Iranian ports before peace talks could restart.
Then, just ahead of the closing bell on Wall Street, Iran’s Tasnim News Agency said Iran would not attend the talks, citing an Iranian official. Soon after, the Associated Press said Vance had called off his trip, citing a U.S. official.
Trump, who first announced the ceasefire on April 7 at 18:32 ET (22:32 GMT), said in a social media post on Tuesday that Iran had broken the truce "numerous times," but did not elaborate further or provide more details. Yet he later told CNBC that he expects the U.S. to make a "great deal" with Iran, adding that the U.S. is in a "very strong negotiating position."  
Trump has seemingly poured cold water on the potential for an extension to the ceasefire, telling CNBC that he does not want to renew the truce. Trump has been insisting that he wants an agreement that will stem ructions in financial markets, along with a promise from Iran not to pursue development of a nuclear weapon.
Elsewhere, in a post on X on Tuesday, the Pentagon said it had boarded a sanctioned tanker without incident in the Indo-Pacific region, noting that it was part of an effort to disrupt ships believed to be supporting Iran.
Also potentially further complicating the situation, Israel’s national military accused Hezbollah of launching "several rockets" in southern Lebanon, saying it constituted a "blatant violation" of the ongoing ceasefire between their countries.   
Impasse over Hormuz as Iran calls blockade ’act of war’
Tehran has moved to shut down the Strait of Hormuz, a conduit off of Iran’s southern coast through which roughly a fifth of the world’s oil passes. A weekslong closure of the strait has caused a spike in oil prices, leading to worries over inflationary pressures in countries around the world and slowing global growth.
Armed with this leverage, Iran has been pushing to forge a deal that both avoids a restart to U.S. strikes and eases sanctions on its nuclear ambitions.
Last week, Iran said it had reopened the strait to commercial shipping traffic, propelling hopes for a lasting peace deal, sending stocks sharply higher and sparking a cratering in oil prices.
But over the weekend, the U.S. announced the seizure of an Iranian-flagged cargo ship attempting to bypass an American military blockade of Iran’s ports and coast, leading to threats of retaliation from Iran. Speaking to CNBC, Trump called the blockade a "tremendous success," arguing that U.S. now "totally controls" the strait.
Iran, for its part, has stressed that the Strait of Hormuz is once again closed because of the ongoing blockade. 
"Blockading Iranian ports is an act of war and thus a violation of the ceasefire. Striking a commercial vessel and taking its crew hostage is an even greater violation," Iran’s foreign minister Abbas Araghchi said on X on Tuesday.
U.S. stocks ended lower, while oil prices edged up. On Monday, the rally in stocks on Wall Street cooled and crude prices stayed well above pre-war levels.
Anuron Mitra contributed to this article
